# python-acoustics

[![Build Status](https://travis-ci.org/python-acoustics/python-acoustics.svg?branch=master)](https://travis-ci.org/python-acoustics/python-acoustics)

The `python-acoustics` module is a Python module with useful tools for acousticians.

## Installation

The latest release can be found on PyPI and installed with `pip install acoustics`.
Otherwise, you can clone this repository and install with `pip install` or `pip install -e` when you want an editable install.


## Examples

Several examples can be found in the `examples` folder.

## Tests

The test suite can be run with

`pytest`

## Documentation

Documentation can be found [online](http://python-acoustics.github.io/python-acoustics/).

## License

`python-acoustics` is distributed under the BSD 3-clause license. See `LICENSE` for more information.

## Contributing

Contributors are always welcome.

functions.name: tests.standards.test_iec_61672_1_2013.test_fast_level
documentation:
Test whether integration with time-constant FAST gives the correct level.

Note that the reference sound pressure is used.

In this test the amplitude of the sine is 1, which means the mean squared \$MS\$ is 0.5
With a reference pressure \$p_r\$ of 2.0e-5 the level should be 91 decibel

$$
L = 10 \cdot \log_{10}{\left(\frac{MS}{p_r^2} \right)}
$$

$$
L = 10 \cdot \log_{10}{\left(\frac{0.5}{(2e-5)^2} \right)} = 91
$$

functions.name: tests.test_signal.test_bandpass
documentation: def test_instantaneous_frequency(amplitude, frequency, fs):
duration = 5.0
samples = int(fs*duration)
t = np.arange(samples) / fs
signal = amplitude * np.sin(2.0*np.pi*frequency*t)
out = instantaneous_frequency(signal, fs)
# Rounding is necessary. We take the first element because occasionally
# there is also a zero.
frequency_determined = np.unique(np.round(out), 0)
assert( frequency == frequency_determined )

modules.name: acoustics.standards.iso_1996_1_2003
documentation:
ISO 1996-1:2003
===============

ISO 1996-1:2003 defines the basic quantities to be used for the description of
noise in community environments and describes basic assessment procedures. It
also specifies methods to assess environmental noise and gives guidance on
predicting the potential annoyance response of a community to long-term exposure
from various types of environmental noises. The sound sources can be separate or
in various combinations. Application of the method to predict annoyance response
is limited to areas where people reside and to related long-term land uses.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_1996_1_2003.composite_rating_level
documentation:
Composite rating level.

```
params levels

Level per period.

params hours

Amount of hours per period.

params adjustment

Adjustment per period.
```

Composite whole-day rating levels are calculated as

$$
L_R = 10 \log{\left[ \sum_i \frac{d_i}{24} 10^{(L_i+K_i)/10}  \right]}
$$

where $i$ is a period. See equation 6 and 7 of the standard.

:::{note}
Summation is done over the last axis.
:::

functions.name: acoustics.imaging.plot_octave
documentation:
Plot octave bands from `data` levels and `octaves` bands.

data: levels in an 1-D NumPy array.

octaves: octaves in an 1-D NumPy array. Note that you can use
{func}`trompe.utils.bands.octave` for this or manually enter all bands.

axes: a `matplotlib.axes` object (optional).

kHz: if `True` it shows "1k" or "2k" instead of "1000" or "2000" as
tick labels (`False` by default, optional).

xlabel: a `str` containing label for x axis (optional).

ylabel: a `str` containing label for y axis (optional).

title: a `str` containing title (optional).

separator: a `str` defining the decimal separator. By default takes '.'
or ',' values according to system settings (when separator is None).

modules.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993
documentation:
ISO 9613-1:1993
===============

ISO 9613-1:1993 specifies an analytical method of calculating the attenuation of sound
as a result of atmospheric absorption for a variety of meteorological conditions.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993.soundspeed
documentation:
Speed of sound $c$.

```
param temperature

Ambient temperature 

param reference_temperature

Reference temperature 
```

The speed of sound is calculated using

$$
c = 343.2 \left( \frac{T}{T_0} \right)
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993.saturation_pressure
documentation:
Saturation vapour pressure $p_{sat}$.

```
param temperature

Ambient temperature 

param reference_pressure

Reference pressure 

param triple_temperature

Triple point temperature water 
```

The saturation vapour pressure is calculated using

$$
p_{sat} = 10^C \cdot p_r
$$

with exponent $C$ given by

$$
C = -6.8346 \cdot \left( \frac{T_{01}}{T} \right)^{1.261}  + 4.6151
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993.molar_concentration_water_vapour
documentation:
Molar concentration of water vapour $h$.

```
param relative_humidity

Relative humidity 

param saturation_pressure

Saturation pressure 

param pressure

Ambient pressure 
```

The molar concentration of water vapour is calculated using

$$
h = h_r  \frac{p_{sat}}{p_a}
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993.relaxation_frequency_oxygen
documentation:
Relaxation frequency of oxygen $f_{r,O}$.

```
param pressure

Ambient pressure 

param reference_pressure

Reference pressure 

param h

Molar concentration of water vapour 
```

The relaxation frequency of oxygen is calculated using

$$
f_{r,O} = \frac{p_a}{p_r} \left( 24 + 4.04 \cdot 10^4 h \frac{0.02 + h}{0.391 + h}  \right)
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993.relaxation_frequency_nitrogen
documentation:
Relaxation frequency of nitrogen $f_{r,N}$.

```
param pressure

Ambient pressure 

param temperature

Ambient temperature 

param h

Molar concentration of water vapour 

param reference_pressure

Reference pressure 

param reference_temperature

Reference temperature 
```

The relaxation frequency of nitrogen is calculated using

$$
f_{r,N} = \frac{p_a}{p_r} \left( \frac{T}{T_0} \right)^{-1/2} \cdot \left( 9 + 280 h \exp{\left\{ -4.170 \left[ \left(\frac{T}{T_0} \right)^{-1/3} -1 \right] \right\} } \right)
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61260_1_2014.exact_center_frequency
documentation:
Center frequencies $f_m$ for band indices $x$. See equation 2 and 3.

```
param x

Band index .

param ref

Reference center frequency .

param fraction

Bandwidth designator :math`b`. For example, for 1/3-octave filter b=3.

param G

Octave frequency ratio .
```

The center frequencies are given by

$$
f_m = f_r G^{x/b}
$$

In case the bandwidth designator $b$ is an even number, the center frequencies are given by

$$
f_m = f_r G^{(2x+1)/2b}
$$

See equation 2 and 3 of the standard.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61260_1_2014.lower_frequency
documentation:
Lower band-edge frequencies. See equation 4.

```
param center

Center frequencies .

param fraction

Bandwidth designator .

param G

Octave frequency ratio .
```

The lower band-edge frequencies are given by

$$
f_1 = f_m G^{-1/2b}
$$

See equation 4 of the standard.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61260_1_2014.upper_frequency
documentation:
Upper band-edge frequencies. See equation 5.

```
param center

Center frequencies .

param fraction

Bandwidth designator .

param G

Octave frequency ratio .
```

The upper band-edge frequencies are given by

$$
f_2 = f_m G^{+1/2b}
$$

See equation 5 of the standard.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61260_1_2014.index_of_frequency
documentation:
Determine the band index `x` from a given frequency.

```
param frequency

Frequencies .

param fraction

Bandwidth designator .

param ref

Reference frequency.

param G

Octave frequency ratio .
```

The index of the center frequency is given by

$$
x = round{b \frac{\log{f/f_{ref} }}{\log{G} }}
$$

:::{note}
This equation is not part of the standard. However, it follows from {func}`exact_center_frequency`.
:::

modules.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61672_1_2013
documentation:
# IEC 61672-1:2013

IEC 61672-1:2013 gives electroacoustical performance specifications for three
kinds of sound measuring instruments [^cite_iec61672]:

- time-weighting sound level meters that measure exponential-time-weighted, frequency-weighted sound levels;
- integrating-averaging sound level meters that measure time-averaged, frequency-weighted sound levels; and
- integrating sound level meters that measure frequency-weighted sound exposure levels.

[^cite_iec61672]: <http://webstore.iec.ch/webstore/webstore.nsf/artnum/048669!opendocument>

## Weighting functions

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_function_a
```

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_function_c
```

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_function_z

```

## Weighting systems

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_system_a
```

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_system_c
```

```
.. autofunction:: weighting_system_z
```

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61672_1_2013.average
documentation:
Average the sound pressure squared.

```
param data

Energetic quantity, e.g. .

param sample_frequency

Sample frequency.

param averaging_time

Averaging time.

returns

```

Time weighting is applied by applying a low-pass filter with one real pole at $-1/\tau$.

:::{note}
Because $f_s \cdot t_i$ is generally not an integer, samples are discarded.
This results in a drift of samples for longer signals (e.g. 60 minutes at 44.1 kHz).
:::

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61672_1_2013.weighting_function_a
documentation:
A-weighting function in decibel.

```
param frequencies

Vector of frequencies at which to evaluate the weighting.

returns

Vector with scaling factors.
```

The weighting curve is

$$
20 \log_{10}{\frac{(f_4^2 * f^4)}{(f^2 + f_1^2) \sqrt{(f^2 + f_2^2)(f^2 + f_3^2)}(f^2 + f_4^2)}} - A_{1000}
$$

with $A_{1000} = -2$ dB.

See equation E.6 of the standard.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61672_1_2013.weighting_function_c
documentation:
C-weighting function in decibel.

```
param frequencies

Vector of frequencies at which to evaluate the weighting.

returns

Vector with scaling factors.
```

The weighting curve is

$$
20 \log_{10}{\frac{(f_4^2 f^2)}{(f^2+f_1^2)(f^2+f_4^2)}} - C_{1000}
$$

with $C_{1000} = -0.062$ dB

See equation E.1 of the standard.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iec_61672_1_2013.weighting_function_z
documentation:

```
param frequencies

modules.name: acoustics.doppler
documentation:
Doppler
=======

Doppler shift module.

functions.name: acoustics.doppler.velocity_from_doppler_shift
documentation:
Calculate velocity based on measured frequency shifts due to Doppler shift.

```
param c

Speed of sound .

param f1

Lower frequency .

param f2

Upper frequency .
```

$$
v = c \cdot \left( \frac{f_2 - f_1}{f_2 + f_1} \right)
$$

The assumption is made that the velocity is constant between the observation times.

functions.name: acoustics.doppler.frequency_shift
documentation:
Frequency shift due to Doppler effect.

```
param frequency

Emitted frequency .

param velocity_source

Velocity of source .
Positive if the source is moving away from the receiver (and negative in the other direction).

param velocity_receiver

Velocity of receiver .
Positive if the receiver is moving towards the source (and negative in the other direction);

param soundspeed

Speed of sound .
```

$$
f = \frac{c + v_r}{c + v_s} f_0
$$

modules.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007
documentation:
# ISO/TR 25417 2007

ISO/TR 25417:2007 specifies definitions of acoustical quantities and terms used
in noise measurement documents prepared by ISO Technical Committee TC 43,
Acoustics, Subcommittee SC 1, Noise, together with their symbols and units, with
the principal aim of harmonizing the terminology used [^cite_iso25417].

[^cite_iso24517]: <http://www.iso.org/iso/home/store/catalogue_tc/catalogue_detail.htm?csnumber=42915>

```
.. inheritance-diagram:: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007
```

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.sound_pressure_level
documentation:
Sound pressure level $L_p$ in dB.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param reference_pressure

Reference value .
```

$$
L_p = 10 \log_{10}{ \left( \frac{p^2}{p_0^2} \right)}
$$

See section 2.2.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.equivalent_sound_pressure_level
documentation:
Time-averaged sound pressure level $L_{p,T}$ or equivalent-continious sound pressure level $L_{p,eqT}$ in dB.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param reference_pressure

Reference value .

param axis

Axis.
```

$$
L_{p,T} = L_{p,eqT} = 10.0 \log_{10}{ \left( \frac{\frac{1}{T} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p^2 (t) \mathrm{d} t  }{p_0^2} \right)}
$$

See section 2.3.

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.max_sound_pressure_level
documentation:
Maximum time-averaged sound pressure level $L_{F,max}$ in dB.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param reference_pressure

Reference value .

param axis

Axis.
```

$$
\mathrm{max}{(L_{p})}
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.peak_sound_pressure
documentation:
Peak sound pressure $p_{peak}$ is the greatest absolute sound pressure during a certain time interval.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param axis

Axis.
```

$$
p_{peak} = \mathrm{max}(|p|)
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.peak_sound_pressure_level
documentation:
Peak sound pressure level $L_{p,peak}$ in dB.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param reference_pressure

Reference value .

param axis

Axis.
```

$$
L_{p,peak} = 10.0 \log \frac{p_{peak}^2.0}{p_0^2}
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.sound_exposure
documentation:
Sound exposure $E_{T}$.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param fs

Sample frequency .

param axis

Axis.
```

$$
E_T = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p^2 (t) \mathrm{d}t
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.sound_exposure_level
documentation:
Sound exposure level $L_{E,T}$ in dB.

```
param pressure

Instantaneous sound pressure .

param fs

Sample frequency .

param sound_exposure

Sound exposure .

param reference_sound_exposure

Reference value 
```

$$
L_{E,T} = 10 \log_{10}{ \frac{E_T}{E_0}  }
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.sound_power_level
documentation:
Sound power level $L_{W}$.

```
param power

Sound power .

param reference_power

Reference sound power .
```

$$
10 \log_{10}{ \frac{P}{P_0}  }
$$

```

Sound power .

$$

documentation:
Sound energy level L\_\{J} in dB.

```
param energy

Sound energy .

param reference_energy

Reference sound energy .
```

$$
L_{J} = 10 \log_{10}{ \frac{J}{J_0} }
$$

functions.name: acoustics.standards.iso_tr_25417_2007.sound_intensity
documentation:
Sound intensity $\mathbf{i}$.

```
param pressure

Sound pressure .

param velocity

Particle velocity .
```

$$
\mathbf{i} = p(t) \cdot \mathbf{u}(t)
$$

documentation:
Time-averaged sound intensity $\mathbf{I}_T$.

```
param intensity

Sound intensity .

Axis.

documentation:
Time-averaged sound intensity level $L_{I,T}$.

```
param time_averaged_sound_intensity

Time-averaged sound intensity .

Reference sound intensity .

$$

Normal time-averaged sound intensity $I_{n,T}$.

$$

modules.name: acoustics.reflection
documentation:
Reflection
==========

The reflection module contains functions for calculating reflection factors and impedances.

functions.name: acoustics.reflection.reflection_factor_plane_wave
documentation:
Plane wave reflection factor $R$.

```
param impedance

Normalized impedance .

param angle

Angle of incidence .
```

The plane wave reflection factor $R$ is given by

$$
R = \frac{Z\cos{\theta}-1}{Z\cos{\theta}+1}
$$

where $Z$ is the normalized impedance and $\theta$ the angle of incidence.

functions.name: acoustics.reflection.numerical_distance
documentation:
Numerical distance $w$.

```
param impedance

Normalized impedance .

param angle

Angle of incidence .

param distance

Path length of the reflected ray .

param wavenumber

Wavenumber .
```

The numerical distance $w$ is given by

$$
w = \sqrt{-j k r  \left( 1 + \frac{1}{Z} \cos{\theta} - \sqrt{1 - \left( \frac{1}{Z} \right)^2} \sin{\theta} \right) }
$$

functions.name: acoustics.reflection.reflection_factor_spherical_wave
documentation:
Spherical wave reflection factor $Q$.

```
param impedance

Normalized impedance .

param angle

Angle of incidence .

param distance

Path length of the reflected ray .

param wavenumber

Wavenumber .
```

The spherical wave relfection factor $Q$ is given by

$$
Q = R \left(1 - R \right) F
$$

where $R$ is the plane wave reflection factor as calculated in {func}`reflection_factor_plane_wave` and $F$ is given by

$$
F = 1 - j \sqrt{ \pi} w e^{-w^2} \mathrm{erfc} \left( j w \right)
$$

where $w$ is the numerical distance as calculated in {func}`numerical_distance`.

functions.name: acoustics.reflection.impedance_delany_and_bazley
documentation:
Normalised specific acoustic impedance according to the empirical one-parameter model by Delany and Bazley.

```
param frequency

Frequency .

param flow_resistivity

Flow resistivity .
```

The impedance $Z$ is given by

$$
Z = 1 + 9.08 \left( \frac{1000f}{\sigma}\right)^{-0.75} - 11.9 j \left( \frac{1000f}{\sigma}\right)^{-0.73}
$$

functions.name: acoustics.reflection.impedance_attenborough
documentation:
Normalised specific acoustics impedance according to the two-parameter model by Attenborough.

```
param frequency

Frequency .

param flow_resistivity

Flow resistivity .

param soundspeed

Speed of sound in air .

param density

Density of air .

param porosity_decrease

Rate of exponential decrease of porosity with depth .

param specific_heat_ratio

Ratio of specific heats  for air.
```

The impedance $Z$ is given by

$$
Z = \frac{\left( 1-j\right) \sqrt{\sigma/f}}{\sqrt{\pi \gamma_0 \rho_0}} - \frac{jc\alpha}{8 \pi \gamma_0 f}
$$

modules.name: acoustics.cepstrum
documentation:
Cepstrum
========

functions.name: acoustics.cepstrum.complex_cepstrum
documentation:
Compute the complex cepstrum of a real sequence.

# Parameters

x

: Real sequence to compute complex cepstrum of.

n

: Length of the Fourier transform.

# Returns

ceps

: The complex cepstrum of the real data sequence `x` computed using the
  Fourier transform.

ndelay

: The amount of samples of circular delay added to `x`.

The complex cepstrum is given by

$$
c[n] = F^{-1}\\left{\\log_{10}{\\left(F{x[n]}\\right)}\\right}
$$

where $x_[n]$ is the input signal and $F$ and :math:F\_\{-1}
are respectively the forward and backward Fourier transform.

# See Also

real_cepstrum: Compute the real cepstrum.
inverse_complex_cepstrum: Compute the inverse complex cepstrum of a real sequence.

# Examples

In the following example we use the cepstrum to determine the fundamental
frequency of a set of harmonics. There is a distinct peak at the quefrency
corresponding to the fundamental frequency. To be more precise, the peak
corresponds to the spacing between the harmonics.

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>>> from acoustics.cepstrum import complex_cepstrum
```

```pycon
>>> duration = 5.0
>>> fs = 8000.0
>>> samples = int(fs*duration)
>>> t = np.arange(samples) / fs
```

```pycon
>>> fundamental = 100.0
>>> harmonics = np.arange(1, 30) * fundamental
>>> signal = np.sin(2.0*np.pi*harmonics[:,None]*t).sum(axis=0)
>>> ceps, _ = complex_cepstrum(signal)
```

```pycon
>>> fig = plt.figure()
>>> ax0 = fig.add_subplot(211)
>>> ax0.plot(t, signal)
>>> ax0.set_xlabel('time in seconds')
>>> ax0.set_xlim(0.0, 0.05)
>>> ax1 = fig.add_subplot(212)
>>> ax1.plot(t, ceps)
>>> ax1.set_xlabel('quefrency in seconds')
>>> ax1.set_xlim(0.005, 0.015)
>>> ax1.set_ylim(-5., +10.)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wikipedia, "Cepstrum".
    <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cepstrum>

[^footnote-2]: M.P. Norton and D.G. Karczub, D.G.,
    "Fundamentals of Noise and Vibration Analysis for Engineers", 2003.

[^footnote-3]: B. P. Bogert, M. J. R. Healy, and J. W. Tukey:
    "The Quefrency Analysis of Time Series for Echoes: Cepstrum, Pseudo
    Autocovariance, Cross-Cepstrum and Saphe Cracking".
    Proceedings of the Symposium on Time Series Analysis
    Chapter 15, 209-243. New York: Wiley, 1963.

functions.name: acoustics.cepstrum.real_cepstrum
documentation:
Compute the real cepstrum of a real sequence.

x

: Real sequence to compute real cepstrum of.

n

: Length of the Fourier transform.

# Returns

ceps: ndarray

: The real cepstrum.

The real cepstrum is given by

$$
c[n] = F^{-1}\left{\log_{10}{\left|F{x[n]}\right|}\right}
$$

where $x_[n]$ is the input signal and $F$ and :math:F\_\{-1}
are respectively the forward and backward Fourier transform. Note that
contrary to the complex cepstrum the magnitude is taken of the spectrum.

# See Also

complex_cepstrum: Compute the complex cepstrum of a real sequence.
inverse_complex_cepstrum: Compute the inverse complex cepstrum of a real sequence.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from acoustics.cepstrum import real_cepstrum
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wikipedia, "Cepstrum".
    <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cepstrum>

functions.name: acoustics.cepstrum.inverse_complex_cepstrum
documentation:
Compute the inverse complex cepstrum of a real sequence.

ceps

: Real sequence to compute inverse complex cepstrum of.

ndelay: int

: The amount of samples of circular delay added to `x`.

# Returns

x

: The inverse complex cepstrum of the real sequence `ceps`.

The inverse complex cepstrum is given by

$$
x[n] = F^{-1}\left{\exp(F(c[n]))\right}
$$

where $c_[n]$ is the input signal and $F$ and :math:F\_\{-1}
are respectively the forward and backward Fourier transform.

# See Also

complex_cepstrum: Compute the complex cepstrum of a real sequence.
real_cepstrum: Compute the real cepstrum of a real sequence.

# Examples

Taking the complex cepstrum and then the inverse complex cepstrum results
in the original sequence.

```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from acoustics.cepstrum import inverse_complex_cepstrum
>>> x = np.arange(10)
>>> ceps, ndelay = complex_cepstrum(x)
>>> y = inverse_complex_cepstrum(ceps, ndelay)
>>> print(x)
>>> print(y)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wikipedia, "Cepstrum".
    <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cepstrum>

The room acoustics module contains several functions to calculate the reverberation time in spaces.

functions.name: acoustics.room.t60_sabine
documentation:
Reverberation time according to Sabine.

```
param surfaces

Surface of the room .
NumPy array that contains different surfaces.

type surfaces



param alpha

Absorption coefficient of the room .
Contains absorption coefficients of surfaces.
It could be one value or some values in different bands (1D and 2D
array, respectively).

type alpha



param volume

Volume of the room .

type volume



param c

Speed of sound .

type c



returns

Reverberation time 
```

Sabine's formula for the reverberation time is:

$$
T_{60} = \frac{24 \ln(10)}{c} \frac{V}{S\alpha}
$$

functions.name: acoustics.room.t60_eyring
documentation:

```
param surfaces

param alpha

Mean absorption coefficient  or by frequency bands

param volume

param c

Speed of sound .

returns

Reverberation time 
```

Eyring's formula for the reverberation time is:

$$
T_{60} = \frac{24 \ln{10} V}{c \left( 4 mV - S \ln{\left( 1 - \alpha \right)} \right)}
$$

modules.name: acoustics.generator
documentation:
# Generator

The generator module provides signal generators.

The following functions calculate `N` samples and return an array containing the samples.

For indefinitely long iteration over the samples, consider using the output of these functions
in {func}`itertools.cycle`.

## Noise

Different types of noise are available. The following table lists the color
of noise and how the power and power density change per octave.

| Color  | Power | Power density |
| ------ | ----- | ------------- |
| White  | +3 dB | 0 dB          |
| Pink   | 0 dB  | -3 dB         |
| Blue   | +6 dB | +3 dB         |
| Brown  | -3 dB | -6 dB         |
| Violet | +9 dB | +6 dB         |

The colored noise is created by generating pseudo-random numbers using
{func}`np.random.randn` and then multiplying these with a curve tyical for the color.
Afterwards, an inverse DFT is performed using {func}`np.fft.irfft`.
Finally, the noise is normalized using {func}`acoustics.signal.normalize`.

### All colors

```
.. autofunction:: noise
```

```
.. autofunction:: noise_generator
```

### Per color

```
.. autofunction:: white
```

```
.. autofunction:: pink
```

```
.. autofunction:: blue
```

```
.. autofunction:: brown
```

```
.. autofunction:: violet

```

## Other

```
.. autofunction:: heaviside
```

For related functions, check {mod}`scipy.signal`.

# Atmosphere

The atmosphere module contains functions and classes related to atmospheric
acoustics and is based on {mod}`acoustics.standards.iso_9613_1_1993`.

functions.name: acoustics.atmosphere.frequency_response
documentation:
Single-sided frequency response.

```
param atmosphere

Atmosphere.

param distance

Distance between source and receiver.

param frequencies

Frequencies for which to compute the response.

param inverse

Whether the attenuation should be undone.
```

functions.name: acoustics.atmosphere.impulse_response
documentation:
Impulse response of sound travelling through `atmosphere` for a given `distance` sampled at `fs`.

```
param atmosphere

Atmosphere.

param distance

Distance between source and receiver.

param fs

Sample frequency

param ntaps

Amount of taps.

param inverse

Whether the attenuation should be undone.
```

The attenuation is calculated for a set of positive frequencies. Because the
attenuation is the same for the negative frequencies, we have Hermitian
symmetry. The attenuation is entirely real-valued. We like to have a constant
group delay and therefore we need a linear-phase filter.

This function creates a zero-phase filter, which is the special case of a
linear-phase filter with zero phase slope. The type of filter is non-causal. The
impulse response of the filter is made causal by rotating it by M/2 samples and
discarding the imaginary parts. A real, even impulse response corresponds to a
real, even frequency response.

modules.name: acoustics.directivity
documentation:
# Directivity

The directivity module provides tools to work with directivity.

The following conventions are used within this module:

- The inclination angle $\theta$ has a range $[0, \pi]$.
- The azimuth angle $\phi$ has a range $[0 , 2 \pi]$.

$$
\theta = \arccos{\frac{z}{r}}
$$

$$
\phi = \arccos{\frac{y}{x}}

functions.name: acoustics.ambisonics.acn
documentation:
Spherical harmonic degree `n` and order `m` for ambisonics order `order`.

```
param order

Ambisonics order.
```

Yields tuples `(n, m)` where `n` is the degree and `m` the order.

Follows ACN.

| ACN | n   | m   | letter |
| --- | --- | --- | ------ |
| 0   | 0   | 0   | W      |
| 1   | 1   | -1  | Y      |
| 2   | 1   | 0   | Z      |
| 3   | 1   | +1  | X      |

Plot scaleogram

print level

functions.name: acoustics.signal.convolve
documentation:
Perform convolution of signal with linear time-variant system `ltv`.

```
param signal

Vector representing input signal .

param ltv

2D array where each column represents an impulse response

param mode

'full', 'valid', or 'same'. See  for an explanation of the options.
```

The convolution of two sequences is given by

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{t} \star \mathbf{u}
$$

This can be written as a matrix-vector multiplication

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{u}
$$

where $T$ is a Toeplitz matrix in which each column represents an impulse response.
In the case of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system, each column represents a time-shifted copy of the first column.
In the time-variant case (LTV), every column can contain a unique impulse response, both in values as in size.

This function assumes all impulse responses are of the same size.
The input matrix `ltv` thus represents the non-shifted version of the Toeplitz matrix.

:::{seealso}
{func}`np.convolve`, {func}`scipy.signal.convolve` and {func}`scipy.signal.fftconvolve` for convolution with LTI system.
:::

documentation:
Calculate window scaling factor.

```

When analysing broadband (filtered noise) signals it is common to normalize
the windowed signal so that it has the same power as the un-windowed one.

$$

$$

documentation:

```

The amplitude spectrum gives the amplitudes of the sinusoidal the signal is built
up from, and the RMS (root-mean-square) amplitudes can easily be found by dividing
these amplitudes with $\sqrt{2}$.

The amplitude spectrum is double-sided.

functions.name: acoustics.signal.auto_spectrum
documentation:
Auto-spectrum of instantaneous signal $x(t)$.

```
param x

Instantaneous signal .

param fs

Sample frequency .

param N

Amount of FFT bins.
```

The auto-spectrum contains the squared amplitudes of the signal. Squared amplitudes
are used when presenting data as it is a measure of the power/energy in the signal.

$$
S_{xx} (f_n) = \overline{X (f_n)} \cdot X (f_n)
$$

The auto-spectrum is double-sided.

The power spectrum, or single-sided autospectrum, contains the squared RMS amplitudes of the signal.

A power spectrum is a spectrum with squared RMS values. The power spectrum is
calculated from the autospectrum of the signal.

functions.name: acoustics.signal.decimate
documentation:
Downsample the signal by using a filter.

By default, an order 8 Chebyshev type I filter is used. A 30 point FIR
filter with hamming window is used if `ftype` is 'fir'.

# Parameters

x

: The signal to be downsampled, as an N-dimensional array.

q

: The downsampling factor.

n

: The order of the filter (1 less than the length for 'fir').

ftype

: The type of the lowpass filter.

axis

: The axis along which to decimate.

zero_phase

: Prevent phase shift by filtering with `filtfilt` instead of `lfilter`.

# Returns

y

: The down-sampled signal.

# See also

resample

# Notes

The `zero_phase` keyword was added in 0.17.0.
The possibility to use instances of `lti` as `ftype` was added in 0.17.0.

functions.name: acoustics.signal.impulse_response_real_even
documentation:
The impulse response of a real and even frequency response is also real and even.

```
param tf

Real and even frequency response. Only positive frequencies.

param ntaps

Amount of taps.

returns

A real and even (double-sided) impulse response with length .
```

A symmetric impulse response is needed. The center of symmetry determines the delay
of the filter and thereby whether the filter is causal (delay>0, linear-phase) or
non-causal (delay=0, linear-phase, zero-phase).

Creating linear phase can be done by multiplying the magnitude with a complex
exponential corresponding to the desired shift. Another method is to rotate the
impulse response.

<https://ccrma.stanford.edu/~jos/filters/Zero_Phase_Filters_Even_Impulse.html>

functions.name: acoustics.signal.linear_phase
documentation:
Compute linear phase delay for a single-sided spectrum.

```
param ntaps

Amount of filter taps.

param steepness

Steepness of phase delay. Default value is 1, corresponding to delay in samples of .

returns

Linear phase delay.
```

A linear phase delay can be added to an impulse response using the function `np.fft.ifftshift`.
Sometimes, however, you would like to add the linear phase delay to the frequency response instead.
This function computes the linear phase delay which can be multiplied with a single-sided frequency response.
